refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work performed.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint or acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The system's functional role is to provide visibility into energy consumption in a form that allows stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality) to assess whether operational practices meet the target. The target achievement is a validation concern dependent on operational execution, not system design. This refinement keeps the FR in the problem domain (what must be measured and communicated) and removes the solution-dependent assumption that the system itself 'achieves' the target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (e.g., fuel/electrical input) and work output metrics (e.g., tons moved, distance traveled, cycle count) during operation, and that these data are accessible in a form that allows calculation of energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved. Confirm that reporting interfaces (dashboard, API, log export) present this metric to authorized stakeholders without loss of fidelity.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate and store the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work) for each operational session.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide access to historical energy efficiency data to authorized stakeholders for analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument the energy source (fuel/power input) with continuous measurement and logging; instrument the work output (tonnage moved) with independent measurement and logging; compute energy_per_ton as a derived metric post-collection.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors into the equipment control loop; calculate energy_per_ton continuously during operation and store snapshots at defined intervals.

DP3: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: (A) energy metering at the power distribution point (centralized), (B) work quantification via independent load/displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio offline.

DP4: Integrate energy and work measurement into a unified telemetry module that samples both streams at synchronized intervals, applies calibration transforms, and emits energy_per_ton as a primary output metric.

DP5: Decompose measurement into two independent subsystems—(A) energy accounting via power draw instrumentation, (B) work accounting via task-completion counters or load cells—with asynchronous data fusion at a reporting layer.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Establish a dual-channel measurement architecture: (A) energy metering at the power distribution point (centralized), (B) work quantification via independent load/displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps and compute ratio offline.

potential_sources: Timestamp synchronization drift between energy and work channels could introduce systematic error in ratio calculation if clock sources are not disciplined.
Sensor calibration drift over the evaluation period may cause energy_per_ton to drift; mitigation requires periodic recalibration protocol.
Offline computation introduces latency; if operational feedback is required, this DP may need augmentation with a real-time compute layer.
Independence of energy and work measurement channels reduces coupling, but data fusion logic at reporting layer must handle missing or out-of-order samples robustly.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 achieves low coupling by separating energy measurement (power distribution point, centralized) from work measurement (distributed load/displacement sensors). Each channel can be designed, tested, and maintained independently. Synchronization is a data-layer concern, not a functional coupling. This design supports modularity and allows substitution of sensor technologies without affecting the measurement architecture.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is accessible for instrumentation at a single point (power distribution). RISK: If energy is distributed (e.g., multiple fuel tanks, hybrid sources), centralized metering may be infeasible; mitigation: extend DP3 to multi-point energy aggregation.

ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) can be quantified independently of energy measurement. RISK: If work is inferred from energy (e.g., no load cell available), the two channels become coupled; mitigation: validate that independent work sensors are available or can be retrofitted.

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization to sub-second precision is achievable. RISK: If clocks drift significantly, energy_per_ton ratio becomes unreliable; mitigation: specify clock discipline (NTP, GPS, or hardware sync) and validation protocol.

ASSUMPTION: Offline computation is acceptable for reporting. RISK: If real-time feedback is required for operational control, DP3 must be augmented with a real-time compute module; mitigation: clarify reporting latency requirements.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough to average out transient measurement noise. RISK: Short evaluation windows may amplify sensor noise; mitigation: specify minimum aggregation window and noise filtering strategy.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit instrumentation of energy or work measurement points. RISK: Safety constraints may restrict sensor placement; mitigation: coordinate with safety plan early.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work performed, such that energy_per_ton = (total energy used) / (total tonnage moved) is computed and validated against E_target over the agreed evaluation period, with timestamp synchronization error ≤ [X] seconds and sensor calibration drift ≤ [Y]% per [Z] days. DP3 should be augmented with explicit requirements for: (1) clock synchronization protocol and tolerance, (2) sensor calibration and drift monitoring, (3) data fusion and missing-sample handling logic, (4) reporting latency and aggregation window, and (5) integration with worksite safety and operational policies. Consider introducing a secondary IM layer (DP3a: real-time compute module) if operational feedback on energy_per_ton is required during work execution.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement architecture encompassing energy metering subsystem, work quantification sensor integration, timestamp synchronization logic, and offline ratio computation engine.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit synchronized dual-channel measurements (energy, work, timestamps) and computed efficiency ratios to external monitoring/logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive task parameters and operational context that define measurement windows and synchronization requirements.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional: transmit raw or processed measurement data to remote analysis or cloud-based computation if offline processing is deferred.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the logical home for energy metering (centralized power distribution measurement) and work quantification (load/displacement sensor fusion). Timestamp synchronization and offline ratio computation are core algorithmic functions that belong in the solution layer. Minor diffusion: telemetry export and tasking input are external dependencies, but these are clean interface contracts.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns physical actuators and propulsion; energy metering at power distribution and work quantification via independent sensors are solution-level concerns, not vehicle-specific hardware. Placing DP3 here would scatter measurement logic across vehicle subsystems and create tight coupling to vehicle architecture.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a command/control interface layer. Dual-channel measurement architecture is a data acquisition and processing concern orthogonal to remote operation. Placing DP3 here would conflate control logic with measurement logic and create unnecessary coupling to operator interface.

Poor fit: Safety Services owns hazard detection and mitigation. While energy/work measurements may inform safety thresholds, the measurement architecture itself (dual-channel synchronization, offline ratio computation) is not a safety-specific function. Placing DP3 here would bloat Safety Services with non-safety measurement logic.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of DP3 because it is responsible for the overall energy-to-work efficiency analysis and optimization. Centralizing energy metering at the power distribution point and integrating independent load/displacement sensors are core measurement infrastructure decisions that belong at the solution architecture level, not scattered across vehicle hardware or control interfaces. Timestamp synchronization and offline ratio computation are algorithmic functions that support the solution's primary objective.
containment_risks: Risk: If the Vehicle module owns the load/displacement sensors as hardware, there may be a physical-logical split where sensor data must be marshaled from Vehicle into NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define a clean sensor data interface (e.g., via Telemetry or a dedicated measurement interface) that decouples sensor ownership from measurement processing.

Risk: If power distribution metering is embedded in a separate power management subsystem (not explicitly listed), DP3 may diffuse across module boundaries. Mitigation: Ensure power metering is logically owned by NGHE Solution even if the physical meter is on the vehicle; use an interface contract to abstract the meter.

Risk: Offline computation may require data buffering and storage; if no persistent storage module is defined, DP3 may leak into infrastructure concerns. Mitigation: Treat offline computation as a batch processing function within NGHE Solution with explicit input/output contracts for data staging.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by establishing a dual-channel measurement architecture (energy metering + work quantification) with synchronized timestamps and offline ratio computation. The DP is solution-focused, technology-neutral, and logically sound. It avoids prescribing implementation details (e.g., specific sensor types, communication protocols) while clearly defining the measurement strategy needed to compute energy_per_ton and verify against E_target acceptance criterion.

diffusion_risks: Physical-logical split risk: Load/displacement sensors may be physically owned/mounted on the Vehicle module, but measurement processing logic (synchronization, ratio computation) must reside in NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Define a clean sensor data interface contract (e.g., timestamped sensor readings via Telemetry or dedicated measurement API) that decouples hardware ownership from algorithmic ownership.

Power distribution metering ownership ambiguity: If power metering hardware is embedded in a separate power management or electrical subsystem not explicitly listed, DP3 logic may diffuse across module boundaries. Mitigation: Establish explicit ownership: NGHE Solution owns the metering logic and data processing; the physical meter is a hardware dependency with a defined interface contract.

Offline computation infrastructure leak: Offline ratio computation requires data buffering, staging, and possibly persistent storage. If no dedicated data persistence or batch processing module is defined, DP3 may leak into infrastructure concerns. Mitigation: Treat offline computation as a contained batch processing function within NGHE Solution with explicit input/output contracts for data staging and archival.

Timestamp synchronization coupling: Synchronizing timestamps across dual channels (energy meter + work sensors) may require access to a shared clock or time service. If this service is not explicitly owned, DP3 may create hidden dependencies. Mitigation: Define timestamp synchronization as an internal NGHE Solution function with a clear contract for time source input (e.g., vehicle system clock, GPS time, or local oscillator).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership candidate with a containment score of 0.92 and diffusion score of 0.08. The module boundary is well-defined: it encompasses energy metering subsystem, work quantification sensor integration, timestamp synchronization logic, and offline ratio computation engine. External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link out) are clean and do not leak DP3 logic. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules are correctly excluded (containment scores 0.45, 0.3, 0.35 respectively) because they own orthogonal concerns (actuators/propulsion, command/control, hazard mitigation). The DP-to-IM mapping is sound provided the identified diffusion risks are mitigated via explicit interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target' is directly computable from the dual-channel measurements (energy_used from power metering, ton_moved from work quantification) that NGHE Solution will produce. Offline ratio computation ensures deterministic, auditable calculation. Measurement windows and synchronization requirements are defined via the Tasking interface, enabling repeatable evaluation over the agreed period. No hidden dependencies or unmeasurable requirements.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to a single, well-scoped IM (NGHE Solution) with explicit responsibilities: (1) acquire synchronized energy and work measurements, (2) compute efficiency ratio offline, (3) export results via Telemetry. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous. Sensor data acquisition and power metering are logically owned by NGHE Solution even if hardware is distributed; interface contracts abstract physical location. No allocation ambiguity or orphaned requirements.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define the sensor data interface contract: specify the format, frequency, and timestamp precision of load/displacement sensor readings flowing from Vehicle (or sensor subsystem) into NGHE Solution. Include latency and synchronization tolerance requirements.

Clarify power metering ownership: document whether the power distribution meter is a hardware component owned by Vehicle/Power Management or a logical function owned by NGHE Solution. If split, define the meter data interface (e.g., energy readings, timestamps, units).

Specify offline computation trigger and data staging: define when offline ratio computation is triggered (e.g., end of task, periodic batch, on-demand), how measurement data is buffered/staged, and where intermediate results are stored. Include data retention and archival requirements.

Define timestamp synchronization mechanism: specify the time source (vehicle clock, GPS, local oscillator) and synchronization tolerance (e.g., ±10 ms) required to ensure energy and work measurements are correctly paired. Document any clock drift compensation.

Add traceability to acceptance criterion: explicitly map the offline ratio computation output (energy_per_ton) to the acceptance criterion (≤ E_target) and define the evaluation period, aggregation method (e.g., mean, percentile), and reporting format.

Document external interface contracts: formalize Telemetry (output format, frequency, data schema), Tasking (input parameters, measurement window definition), and Network Link (optional remote transmission conditions) as interface specifications to prevent scope creep.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, indicating the decomposition is logically sound and the DP is well-scoped to NGHE Solution. However, the DP-IM gate identifies six concrete diffusion risks that, while mitigatable, are not yet resolved in the current specification. Given the high safety criticality of energy efficiency measurement (which underpins vehicle performance validation and potentially hazard detection), proceeding to sub-FR derivation without first resolving these interface and ownership ambiguities would propagate uncertainty into lower levels. The recommended revisions are not cosmetic; they establish the interface contracts and ownership boundaries necessary to prevent DP3 logic from leaking across module boundaries during detailed design and implementation. A REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL decision ensures these contracts are explicit before decomposition proceeds, reducing rework and integration risk.

revise.why: DP3 is logically correct but under-specified in terms of interface contracts and ownership boundaries. The six diffusion risks identified in the DP-IM gate (sensor data interface, power metering ownership, offline computation infrastructure, timestamp synchronization, external interface formalization) must be resolved by augmenting DP3 with explicit interface specifications and ownership clarifications. This is a refinement of DP3, not a replacement: the core logic (dual-channel measurement + offline ratio computation) remains unchanged, but the DP must now include or reference interface contracts that prevent diffusion. Recommended approach: (1) Extend DP3 narrative to include interface contract requirements (sensor data format, power meter data interface, timestamp synchronization mechanism, offline computation trigger/staging, Telemetry/Tasking/Network Link interface specifications); (2) Create a companion interface specification document or design artifact that formalizes these contracts; (3) Re-run the DP-IM gate to confirm diffusion risks are mitigated to acceptable levels (target diffusion score ≤ 0.05). Once revised DP3 passes with diffusion score < 0.05, proceed to sub-FR derivation.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership candidate for energy metering logic and work quantification processing (containment score 0.92).

Sensor hardware (load/displacement sensors, power meter) may be physically distributed across Vehicle or Power Management modules, but measurement logic ownership is logically centralized in NGHE Solution.
Offline ratio computation is feasible and preferred over real-time computation for determinism and auditability.
Timestamp synchronization across dual channels is achievable within vehicle system constraints (e.g., ±10 ms tolerance).
Acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton ≤ E_target' is directly computable from dual-channel measurements without hidden dependencies.
Safety criticality of energy efficiency measurement is high because it validates vehicle performance and may inform hazard detection logic.
open_risks: Sensor data interface contract not yet formalized: load/displacement sensor readings flowing from Vehicle into NGHE Solution lack defined format, frequency, timestamp precision, latency, and synchronization tolerance. Risk: integration mismatch, timestamp misalignment, data loss.

Power metering ownership ambiguity: unclear whether power distribution meter is owned by Vehicle/Power Management or NGHE Solution. Risk: diffusion of metering logic across module boundaries, duplicate metering logic, inconsistent data sources.

Offline computation infrastructure not explicitly scoped: data buffering, staging, persistence, and batch processing infrastructure not assigned to a specific module. Risk: DP3 logic leaks into infrastructure concerns, unclear data retention and archival responsibilities.

Timestamp synchronization mechanism not specified: time source (vehicle clock, GPS, local oscillator) and synchronization tolerance not defined. Risk: clock drift, timestamp misalignment, inability to pair energy and work measurements correctly.

External interface contracts not formalized: Telemetry output format, Tasking input parameters, Network Link transmission conditions lack formal specification. Risk: scope creep, integration ambiguity, unclear data schema and reporting format.

Evaluation period and aggregation method not defined: acceptance criterion evaluation window, aggregation method (mean, percentile, max), and reporting format not specified. Risk: ambiguous acceptance criterion, non-repeatable evaluation, disputes over compliance.
evidence_needed: Sensor data interface specification: document format (e.g., JSON, binary, CAN), frequency (Hz), timestamp precision (ms), latency budget (ms), and synchronization tolerance (ms) for load/displacement sensor readings.

Power metering ownership decision: clarify whether power meter is owned by Vehicle/Power Management (with interface contract to NGHE Solution) or by NGHE Solution (with hardware dependency on Vehicle). Document the meter data interface (energy readings, timestamps, units, frequency).

Offline computation design: specify trigger conditions (end of task, periodic batch, on-demand), data buffering strategy (in-memory, persistent storage), staging location, intermediate result storage, and data retention/archival policy.

Timestamp synchronization specification: define time source (vehicle clock, GPS, local oscillator), synchronization mechanism (NTP, PTP, local sync), tolerance (e.g., ±10 ms), and clock drift compensation strategy.
Telemetry interface specification: document output format (schema, encoding), frequency (Hz), data fields (energy_per_ton, measurement window, timestamp, confidence), and transmission protocol.
Tasking interface specification: document input parameters (measurement window start/end, E_target, aggregation method), format, and validation rules.

Acceptance criterion evaluation specification: define evaluation period (e.g., per-task, per-shift, per-day), aggregation method (mean, percentile, max), reporting format, and compliance threshold (≤ E_target).

Safety criticality justification: document why energy efficiency measurement is safety-critical and how it informs hazard detection or performance validation logic. Identify any downstream safety-critical dependencies.
